source_texts: The captain offered me a cup of the still-warm milk. I could not stop myself showing repugnance at this brew. He assured me that the milk was excellent, and no different from cows’ milk.
. .” To Alyosha’s great distress, her face was beaming with joy. Katerina suddenly reappeared. She held two rainbow-colored hundred-ruble notes in her hand.
The Prince produced the twenty-five-rouble note and held it out to Ferdyshchenko. He unfolded it, inspected it, then turned it over and brought it up to the light.
He stood there a moment, took several deep breaths, shook his head, turned around, and did not notice Semyonych† flattened against the wall in a shadowy corner of the corridor.
Leaning over the railing, he shouted something or other in the direction of the vestibule. The shout was answered with silence, and then, a protesting falsetto:

A thousand lords with girdle-jade walk in front and rear. The many flags of guardsmen stand both left and right. Those holding gilt bludgeons, And halberds and axes, March in pairs and pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity. The dragons fly and the phoenixes dance; The falcons soar and the eagles take wing. This Son of Heaven’s an upright sage; The righteous ministers are good. They increase our bliss by a thousand years, surpassing Yu and Shun; They secure peace of ten thousand ages, rivaling Yao and Tang. We also see the curve-handled umbrella, And robes with rolling dragons— Their glare lighting up each other; The jade joined-rings, The phoenix fans, Waving through holy mist. Those caps of pearls and belts of jade; The purple sashes and medals of gold. A thousand rows of soldiers protect the Throne; Two lines of marshals uphold the carriage. This emperor, cleansed and sincere, bows to the Buddha, Glad to raise incense and seek virtue’s fruit. The grand cortege of the Tang emperor soon arrived in front of the temple. The emperor ordered a halt to the music, left the carriages, and led many officials in the worship of Buddha by taking up burning incense sticks in their hands. After bowing three times holding the incense, they raised their heads and looked around them. This was indeed a splendid religious hall. You see Dancing flags and banners; Bright, gleaming sunshades. Dancing flags and banners Fill the air with strands of flashing colored mists. Bright, gleaming sunshades Glow in the sun as fiery bolts. Imposing, the gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha;14 Most awesome, the jade features of the arhats. Divine flowers fill the vases. Sandalwood incense burn in the urns. The divine flowers filling the vases Adorn the temple with a brilliant forest of brocade. The sandalwood incense burning in the urns Covers the clear sky with waves of fragrant clouds. Piled high on red trays are fruits in season. On colored counters, mounds of cakes and sweets rest. Rows of noble priests chant the holy sūtras To save from their travails those orphaned souls. Taizong and his officials each lifted the incense; they also worshipped the golden body of the Buddha and paid homage to the arhats. Thereafter, the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, the Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the various monks to greet the Tang emperor. After the ceremony, they went back to their seats according to their rank and station. The priest then presented Taizong with the proclamation for the deliverance of the orphaned souls. It read: The supreme virtue is vast and endless, for Buddhism is founded upon Nirvāṇa. The spirit of the pure and the clean circulates freely and flows everywhere in the Three Regions. There are a thousand changes and ten thousand transformations, all regulated by the forces of yin and yang. Boundless and vast indeed are the substance, the function, the true nature, and the permanence of such phenomena. But look at those orphaned souls, how worthy they are of our pity and commiseration! Now by the holy command of Taizong, we have selected and assembled various priests, who will engage in meditation and in the proclamation of the Law. Flinging wide the gates of salvation and setting in motion many vessels of mercy, we would deliver you, the multitudes, from the Sea of Woe and save you from perdition and from the Sixfold Path. You will be led to return to the way of truth and to enjoy the bliss of Heaven. Whether it be by motion, rest, or nonactivity, you will be united with, and become, pure essences. Therefore make use of this noble occasion, for you are invited to the pleasures of the celestial city. Take advantage of our Grand Mass so that you may find release from Hell’s confinement, ascend quickly and freely to ultimate bliss, and travel without restraint in the Region of the West.15 The poem says: An urn of immortal incense. Some scrolls of salvific power. As we proclaim this boundless Law, Receive now Heaven’s endless grace. All your guilt and crime abolished, You lost souls may leave your prison. May our nation be firmly blessed With peace long and all-embracing. Highly pleased by what he read, Taizong said to the monks, “Be firm, all of you, in your devotion, and do not slack in your service to Buddha. After the achievement of merit and after each has received his blessing, we shall reward you handsomely. Be assured that you will not have labored in vain.”

“What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”
She occupied, in the Boulevard Haussmann, the second storey of a large new house, the owner of which was content to let to single ladies, in order to get his plaster dried.

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.

“They’re coming, here they are. And the General is making up the rear. I’ll fling open all the doors and call all my daughters, every single one, now, this minute,” Lebedev mumbled softly in a fluster, dashing from door to door.
rationales: Outputs 3 and 4 share striking similarities in sentence structure and word choice. Both use the phrase 'offered me a cup of... milk, which was still warm' and the rare word 'repugnance/repugnance to the drink.' Both include the assurance from the captain that the milk was 'excellent, and not to be distinguished from cow's milk.' These parallelisms in phrasing and vocabulary are strong indicators that they are from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 share similar phrasing and structure: both directly mention Alyosha's dismay, Katerina's face being radiant/happy, and both describe the notes as vibrant and rainbow-colored using almost identical phrases ('vibrant with all the colors of the rainbow', 'vibrant, rainbow-hued'). Both reconstruct the scene in a similar order, mention Katerina's return as sudden, and use comparable vocabulary (e.g. 'returned/rematerialized', 'radiating happiness'). The combination of these stylistic similarities suggests these two were produced by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 both begin with a similar structure: 'The prince [verb] the twenty-five-rouble note from his waistcoat pocket and [action] it to Ferdyshchenko.' Both use the phrase 'unfolded it, looked at it, turned it over, then held it up to the light' (with only minor stylistic differences in the sequencing and conjunctions) and both mention 'waistcoat pocket,' which does not appear in the other outputs. The sentence and event ordering are extremely similar. These shared patterns strongly suggest that 1 and 2 come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share unique structural and stylistic features: both have a repetitive sentence structure ('had stood, breathed, shaken his head, turned round'), use semicolons to link complex ideas, and preserve a sequence about putting head to the keyhole with a burst of commentary on his curiosity at the end. The phrasing regarding the 'master’s son'/'barin' and the idea that 'something wasn't right'/'something was wrong' are highly specific and mirrored in both. The other paraphrases (0, 1, 3) are much more compressed and do not follow this extended, detailed, almost stream-of-consciousness pattern.

Outputs 0 and 2 are stylistically similar in that they both use a simple, direct structure: 'He peered/leaned over the edge/railing and yelled/shouted something toward/in the direction of the entryway/vestibule.' Both paraphrases focus on the same two-part sequence: an action (shouting) and then its response (silence, then a high-pitched voice/protesting falsetto). The phrasing is quite close in these two cases compared to the other outputs, which either introduce more details about the character (like 1 and 4) or elaborate differently (like 3). The similarities in sentence construction, word choices ('shouted something', 'the shout was answered by silence'), and their division into two separate sentences suggest that the same paraphraser generated outputs 0 and 2.

Outputs 1 and 2 both provide detailed, multi-paragraph narratives that structure the events in a sequential and descriptive manner, focusing on a grand procession, the emperor, the temple, the offering ritual, and eventually a proclamation and its poem. Both mention specifics such as jade ornaments, red trays, burning incense, Lokājyeṣṭha, and the deliverance of orphaned souls, using a similar tone, level of detail, and organization. Both even paraphrase the emperor's encouragement and the purpose of the event in highly similar language. The sentence and paragraph structure are also notably parallel, suggesting the same paraphraser. The other outputs differ significantly in style: 0 is a listing format, 3 is briefer and skips details, while 4 is an entirely unrelated narrative not connected to the primary text.

Outputs 2 and 4 share highly similar sentence structures, sequence of ideas, and word choices such as 'What? A priest?'/'What, a priest?', the rhetorical 'It's not needed/necessary', discussion about paying the priest, expressing not having sinned, God forgiving regardless, and the 'He knows how much I've been through/He knows how I have suffered'. The progression of the ideas and the syntax closely match, more so than with the other paraphrases, which are either more interpretive (1, 3) or structurally distinct (0).

Indexes 1 and 3 both describe the apartment situation on boulevard Haussmann in a very similar way, focusing on the owner/landlord's motivation related to drying plaster, and the presence of single women. Their sentence structure, use of phrases like 'letting units to single women' or 'content to let the single ladies...dry his plaster', and coverage of building details are highly similar. In contrast, the other paraphrases (0 and 2) focus entirely on decor and not the living arrangement, while 4, although related, has noticeable stylistic differences. Thus, 1 and 3 are from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share highly distinctive phrasing, such as 'As for the man, he was so tired...' and 'snuffed out his candle with his nostrils, as prisoners do'/'Snuffing out his candle with his nostrils after the manner of convicts.' Both use archaic or literal phrasing uncommon in the other paraphrased versions. Additionally, their sentence structure and the use of phrases like 'fell into a profound sleep' at the end suggest the same paraphraser. The other paraphrases use distinctly modern/contemporary word choices and constructions.

Outputs 1 and 2 share notable stylistic similarities: both keep closely to the original structure and order of details (mentioning 'they’re coming', 'the general is coming behind/after them'), both use direct speech with the same order of events (opening all the doors, calling all girls/daughters), and both describe Lebedev's panic with physical action (rushing or lunging from door to door). Both use similar phrases ('right now, right this minute', 'right now, right now') and similar expressions ('summon all my daughters, all of them' / 'call all the girls, right now'). These textual fingerprints suggest the same paraphraser.

human_par3: The captain offered me a cup of the milk, which was still warm. I could not help showing my repugnance to the drink; but he assured me that it was excellent, and not to be distinguished from cow’s milk.
llama_31_8b: The captain handed me a hot cup of liquid that was the milk of a cow, supplementing his words with assurance that it tasted like regular milk.
gemini_25_flash: The captain presented me with a cup of milk that was still warm. I couldn't help but show my disgust at the drink. He insisted the milk was good and no different from that of cows.
The captain extended a cup of warm milk, which I instinctively recoiled from. He insisted it was perfectly fine and indistinguishable from cow's milk.
dipper_lowmed: The captain offered me a cup of still warm milk. He assured me that the milk was excellent and no different from cows' milk. I could not help feeling repugnance at this beverage.

Alyosha was dismayed to see Katerina's face lit up with happiness. She had returned, holding two hundred-ruble notes, vibrant with all the colors of the rainbow.

The prince took a twenty-five-rouble note from his waistcoat pocket and handed it to Ferdyshchenko. The man unfolded it, looked at it, turned it over, then held it up to the light.

human_par3: He had stood there for a bit, breathed deeply, shaken his head, turned round, without noticing Semyonych, who was pressed into the shadowy corner of the corridor; he had stood there, breathed deeply some more, and then—put his face to the keyhole with its shaft of light: and—how he had stuck to it, unable to detach himself from the door! His curiosity wasn’t proper curiosity for the master’s son, something wasn’t nice about it—it wasn’t right.

Had stood, breathed, shook his head, turned round, not noticing Semyonych, who was pressed up against a shadowy corner of the corridor; had stood, breathed again, and put his head – to the keyhole that let light through: yes – he was glued there, could not take his eyes off the door! The young barin’s curiosity was not barin -like, there was something wrong – something not right …
llama_31_8b: For a brief moment, he stayed still, drawing in several deep breaths before shaking his head. Turning around, he seemed unaware that Semyonych was standing right behind him in the dark corner of the corridor, hidden from view.
gemini_25_flash: For a moment, he paused, taking a few deep breaths and shaking his head. He then turned and walked away, completely unaware of Semyonych, who was pressed against the wall in a dark hallway.
dipper_lowmed: He stood there a moment, took several deep breaths, shook his head, turned around and did not see Semyonitch pressed flat against the wall in a dark corner of the corridor.

human_par3: Leaning over the balustrade, Nikolai Apollonovich called down in the direction of the entrance-hall, but his call was answered first by silence, then, with excessive distinctness, by an unexpected, reluctant falsetto:

Nikolai Apollonovich Ableukhov stood above the staircase balustrade in his little multicoloured robe, scattering in all directions an iridescent gleam, forming a complete contrast to the column and the small alabaster pillar from which a white Niobe raised heavenward her alabaster eyes.
llama_31_8b: He angled over the railing and bellowed a phrase, though he couldn't quite be heard in the vestibule. His words were met only with stillness, but then a high-pitched complaint floated up.
gemini_25_flash: He peered over the edge and yelled something toward the entryway. His shout was met with quiet, followed by a high-pitched, complaining voice:
dipper_lowmed: Leaning over the railing, he shouted something in the direction of the vestibule. The shout was answered by silence, and then by a protesting falsetto:

human_par3: There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
llama_31_8b:
Here's a paraphrased version of the text that preserves the original meaning:

A grand procession approached the temple, with dignitaries and high-ranking officials clad in finery and adorned with symbols of authority. Banners and flags flapped in the wind, while guardsmen marched in formation, their armor glinting in the sunlight. The emperor, known for his compassion and wisdom, led a delegation of officials as they honored the Buddha with offerings and prayers. The temple itself was a site of breathtaking beauty, with intricate golden sculptures and delicate jade features. The air was thick with the scent of sandalwood incense, and exotic flowers bloomed amidst the elegant furnishings. Priests and monks gathered around, chanting sacred texts and seeking to guide souls from the jaws of suffering.

The ceremony concluded with the priest presenting the emperor with a sacred proclamation, calling for the deliverance of orphaned souls. The text proclaimed the boundless virtue of the Buddha and the universal principle of karma, offering words of hope and solace to those seeking liberation. Emphasizing the importance of compassion and devotion, it encouraged all present to seize this opportunity for spiritual rebirth.

The emperor was visibly moved by the message, and in response, he exhorted the monks to remain steadfast in their duty, vowing to reward their dedication to the Buddha with honor and riches. With sincere conviction, he assured the assembled throng that their efforts would not go unrewarded, and that the blessings they sought would indeed be theirs to enjoy.

A magnificent procession unfolds before us. The dignitaries, adorned with jade girdles, lead the way, accompanied by guardsmen carrying flags, halberds, and axes. The air is filled with the rhythmic sound of marching and the soft glow of lanterns. Dragons and phoenixes seem to dance, while falcons and eagles soar through the skies. At the heart of the procession stands the Son of Heaven, an upright and wise ruler, flanked by his ministers, who have brought immense prosperity to the land, rivaling the great emperors of old. The imperial entourage is complete with its attendant umbrellas, jade ornaments, and exquisite robes. Thousands of soldiers protect the emperor's throne, while marshals stand guard over the carriage. As the procession reaches the temple, the emperor, humbled and sincere, offers his respects to the Buddha, burning incense and seeking the blessings of virtue.

Upon arrival, the emperor orders a halt to the music, disembarks, and leads the officials in a ritual of worship, carrying incense sticks and bowing three times. The temple is a magnificent sight, filled with vibrant colors, dancing flags, and shimmering sunshades. The gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha and the jade features of the arhats command awe and reverence. The air is filled with the sweet scent of incense and the divine flowers that adorn the temple. Fruits and sweets are offered on red trays, while cakes and treats lie on colorful counters. The noble priests chant sacred scriptures, seeking to free the trapped spirits of the orphaned souls.

The ceremony comes to a close, and the monks, led by the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, greet the emperor. A proclamation is presented to Taizong, inviting the multitudes to partake in the Grand Mass, offering release from the Sea of Woe and a chance to ascend to ultimate bliss. The poem reminds us of the preciousness of this opportunity, urging all to take advantage of the noble occasion and receive Heaven's endless grace. In response, Taizong expresses his satisfaction, urging the monks to remain devoted and faithful, assuring them of a generous reward for their service to the Buddha.
gemini_25_flash:
A regal procession unfolded: thousands of lords adorned in jade, flanked by guardsmen with flags, gilded bludgeons, halberds, and axes. Red silk lanterns and a royal incense urn moved with solemnity. Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes appeared to dance and fly, while falcons and eagles soared. The emperor, a virtuous and wise ruler, was accompanied by righteous ministers who promised long-lasting peace and prosperity, surpassing legendary times.

The scene continued with elegant, curve-handled umbrellas and robes embroidered with dragons that seemed to glow. Jade rings and phoenix-feather fans swayed through a sacred mist. Caps adorned with pearls and belts of jade, along with purple sashes and gold medals, added to the grandeur. A thousand soldiers protected the emperor, with two lines of marshals guiding his carriage.

This pure-hearted emperor, Taizong, arrived at the temple, bowing to Buddha and gladly offering incense to seek virtue. He ordered the music to stop and, with his officials, stepped out of the carriages to worship. After bowing three times with burning incense, they looked around at the magnificent temple.

The temple was vibrant with dancing flags and banners, which created flashing colored mists, and brightly gleaming sunshades that shone like fiery bolts. Inside, an imposing golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats stood. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a brilliant brocade, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, covering the clear sky with fragrant clouds. Red trays piled high with seasonal fruits and counters laden with cakes and sweets completed the offering. Rows of noble priests chanted holy sūtras, aiming to bring peace to orphaned souls.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also worshipped the golden Buddha and honored the arhats. Afterwards, Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law and Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks in greeting the emperor. Once they returned to their seats, Chen Xuanzang presented Taizong with a proclamation for the deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation spoke of the boundless virtue of Buddhism, founded on Nirvāṇa, and the pervasive purity of its spirit throughout the Three Realms, guided by yin and yang. It expressed pity for orphaned souls and explained that by Taizong's command, various priests would meditate and proclaim the Law. They would open the gates of salvation and offer countless acts of mercy to rescue multitudes from suffering and perdition, guiding them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It encouraged these souls to embrace this opportunity to escape hell's confines, ascend to ultimate happiness, and freely journey to the Western Region.

A poem accompanying the proclamation described an urn of immortal incense and scrolls of salvific power, offering endless heavenly grace and abolishing guilt and crime for lost souls. It prayed for the nation's lasting peace and blessings.

Greatly pleased, Taizong encouraged the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha, promising ample rewards for their efforts and merits, assuring them that their hard work would not be in vain.
dipper_lowmed: Dragons fly and phoenixes dance; Hawks soar and eagles soar, With falcons, eagles, and hawks in pursuit. This virtuous emperor bows to worship Buddha; He sincerely lifts incense to seek blessings. With bludgeons, halberds, and axes, The soldiers march in ranks and files; With spears, swords, and scimitars, The guardsman of the city walks in pairs; The many flags of the guards march in ranks; The halberds, the axes, and the lances, March in pairs; The red silk lanterns, The royal incense urn, Move in solemnity; The hats with the twisted dragons, The gourds of gold, Move in splendor; The swords and scimitars, The curved halberds, Move in celestial splendor; The trays of fruits in season; On colored counters, colored cakes and sweets in season.

human_par3: ‘What’s that? A priest?… Don’t bother… Where are you going to find a spare rouble? … I’ve no sins on me!… God will have to forgive me without that… He knows how I’ve suffered!… And if he won’t forgive me, too bad!…’
llama_31_8b: The person is skeptical about the need for a priest, feeling that they don't have any wrongdoing to confess and that God should forgive them regardless. They suggest that they're already struggling financially and can't afford to pay for a priest's services, and even if they could, they feel that their sufferings are a virtue that should be enough to earn God's forgiveness.

I'm hesitant to seek the help of a priest, we don't have the means to pay for it. But even if I did go, I feel I don't deserve it, as I consider myself sinless. I believe God should be merciful and forgive me out of compassion for all I've endured. If He doesn't, then so be it.
gemini_25_flash: "What, a priest? That's not needed. . . . How would we afford to pay him? I haven't sinned! God should forgive me regardless. . . . He knows how much I've been through! And if He doesn't forgive me, then so be it!"
dipper_lowmed: “What? A priest? It’s not necessary. And where could we find the money to pay him? I have no sins. Even without that God must forgive me. He knows how I have suffered! And if he doesn’t forgive me, so be it!...”

human_par3: The vulgar sumptuosity of gilded consoles and gilded chairs formed a crude contrast therein to the bric-a-brac of a secondhand furniture shop--to mahogany round tables, that is to say, and zinc candelabras, which sought to imitate Florentine bronze.

The flat was far too big for her and had never been completely furnished; the flashy, opulent, gilt chairs and console tables formed a violent contrast to the second-hand junk, mahogany pedestal tables and zinc candelabra pretending to be Florentine bronze.
llama_31_8b: A large, modern building on the Boulevard Haussmann housed her on its second floor, along with other single women, in exchange for allowing the owner to use the space while his plasterwork dried.
gemini_25_flash: She lived on the third floor of a spacious, recently built apartment building on Boulevard Haussmann. The landlord preferred letting units to single women so the plaster could dry out.
dipper_lowmed: On the boulevard Haussmann she occupied the second floor of a large new house, the owner of which was content to let the single ladies in order to dry his plaster.

human_par3: As for the man, he was actually so fatigued that he did not even profit by the nice white sheets. Snuffing out his candle with his nostrils after the manner of convicts, he dropped, all dressed as he was, upon the bed, where he immediately fell into a profound sleep.
llama_31_8b: The man was utterly exhausted, too drained to even appreciate the comfort of the fresh, white sheets. He blew out the candle with a swift movement, a habit often seen in prisoners, and then dropped onto the bed still fully dressed, promptly sinking into a profound sleep.

The man was utterly exhausted and didn't even bother to take advantage of the fresh, white bedding. After blowing out his candle by directing the flame into his nose, a habit often seen among prisoners, he collapsed onto the bed in his clothes and fell into a profound sleep almost instantly.
gemini_25_flash: The man was so utterly exhausted that he barely noticed the fresh, white sheets. He extinguished his candle using his nostril, a technique akin to a prisoner, and without bothering to undress, collapsed onto the bed and instantly drifted into a profound slumber.
dipper_lowmed: He snuffed out his candle with his nostrils, as prisoners do, and threw himself on the bed fully dressed, and immediately fell into a deep sleep. As for the man, he was so tired that he did not even take advantage of these clean white sheets.

human_par3: ‘They’re coming, sir, they’re coming. And even the general is coming, after them. I’m going open all the doors, and summon all my daughters, all of them, right now, right now,’ Lebedev whispered in fear, waving his arms and lunging from one door to the next.
llama_31_8b: Lebedev's voice was barely audible as he exclaimed, "They're here! The General is bringing up the rear! I need to get everyone moving, now!" He hurriedly opened all the doors and called out for his daughters to gather immediately.

Lebedev spoke quietly to himself as he rushed from room to room, saying, "They're approaching, they're here – I need to let all of my daughters know immediately, every single one of them. I'll throw all the doors open right now."
gemini_25_flash: Flustered, Lebedev darted between doorways, quietly muttering, "They're arriving, indeed they are. And the General is bringing up the end. I'll throw open every door and summon all my daughters right this second."
dipper_lowmed: “They’re coming, they’re here, now I’ll open all the doors and call all the girls, right now, right this minute,” Lebedev muttered in a panic, rushing from one door to another. “And the general is coming behind them.”

Katerina suddenly reappeared. She held two rainbow hundred-rouble notes in her hand. To Alyosha’s great distress, her face glowed with joy.
The Prince presented Ferdyshchenko with the twenty-five-rouble note, which he unfolded, examined briefly, flipped over, and then held up to the light to study it more closely.

A magnificent procession unfolds before us. The dignitaries, adorned with jade girdles, lead the way, accompanied by guardsmen carrying flags, halberds, and axes. The air is filled with the rhythmic sound of marching and the soft glow of lanterns. Dragons and phoenixes seem to dance, while falcons and eagles soar through the skies. At the heart of the procession stands the Son of Heaven, an upright and wise ruler, flanked by his ministers, who have brought immense prosperity to the land, rivaling the great emperors of old. The imperial entourage is complete with its attendant umbrellas, jade ornaments, and exquisite robes. Thousands of soldiers protect the emperor's throne, while marshals stand guard over the carriage. As the procession reaches the temple, the emperor, humbled and sincere, offers his respects to the Buddha, burning incense and seeking the blessings of virtue.

Upon arrival, the emperor orders a halt to the music, disembarks, and leads the officials in a ritual of worship, carrying incense sticks and bowing three times. The temple is a magnificent sight, filled with vibrant colors, dancing flags, and shimmering sunshades. The gold image of Lokājyeṣṭha and the jade features of the arhats command awe and reverence. The air is filled with the sweet scent of incense and the divine flowers that adorn the temple. Fruits and sweets are offered on red trays, while cakes and treats lie on colorful counters. The noble priests chant sacred scriptures, seeking to free the trapped spirits of the orphaned souls.

The ceremony comes to a close, and the monks, led by the Master of the Law, Chen Xuanzang, greet the emperor. A proclamation is presented to Taizong, inviting the multitudes to partake in the Grand Mass, offering release from the Sea of Woe and a chance to ascend to ultimate bliss. The poem reminds us of the preciousness of this opportunity, urging all to take advantage of the noble occasion and receive Heaven's endless grace. In response, Taizong expresses his satisfaction, urging the monks to remain devoted and faithful, assuring them of a generous reward for their service to the Buddha.

Leaning over the railing, he shouted something in the direction of the vestibule. The shout was answered by silence, and then by a protesting falsetto:

A regal procession unfolded: thousands of lords adorned in jade, flanked by guardsmen with flags, gilded bludgeons, halberds, and axes. Red silk lanterns and a royal incense urn moved with solemnity. Mythical creatures like dragons and phoenixes appeared to dance and fly, while falcons and eagles soared. The emperor, a virtuous and wise ruler, was accompanied by righteous ministers who promised long-lasting peace and prosperity, surpassing legendary times.

The scene continued with elegant, curve-handled umbrellas and robes embroidered with dragons that seemed to glow. Jade rings and phoenix-feather fans swayed through a sacred mist. Caps adorned with pearls and belts of jade, along with purple sashes and gold medals, added to the grandeur. A thousand soldiers protected the emperor, with two lines of marshals guiding his carriage.

This pure-hearted emperor, Taizong, arrived at the temple, bowing to Buddha and gladly offering incense to seek virtue. He ordered the music to stop and, with his officials, stepped out of the carriages to worship. After bowing three times with burning incense, they looked around at the magnificent temple.

The temple was vibrant with dancing flags and banners, which created flashing colored mists, and brightly gleaming sunshades that shone like fiery bolts. Inside, an imposing golden statue of Lokājyeṣṭha and awe-inspiring jade figures of arhats stood. Vases overflowed with divine flowers, forming a brilliant brocade, and sandalwood incense burned in urns, covering the clear sky with fragrant clouds. Red trays piled high with seasonal fruits and counters laden with cakes and sweets completed the offering. Rows of noble priests chanted holy sūtras, aiming to bring peace to orphaned souls.

Taizong and his officials, after offering incense, also worshipped the golden Buddha and honored the arhats. Afterwards, Chen Xuanzang, the Master of the Law and Grand Expositor of the Faith, led the monks in greeting the emperor. Once they returned to their seats, Chen Xuanzang presented Taizong with a proclamation for the deliverance of orphaned souls.

The proclamation spoke of the boundless virtue of Buddhism, founded on Nirvāṇa, and the pervasive purity of its spirit throughout the Three Realms, guided by yin and yang. It expressed pity for orphaned souls and explained that by Taizong's command, various priests would meditate and proclaim the Law. They would open the gates of salvation and offer countless acts of mercy to rescue multitudes from suffering and perdition, guiding them towards truth and heavenly bliss. It encouraged these souls to embrace this opportunity to escape hell's confines, ascend to ultimate happiness, and freely journey to the Western Region.

A poem accompanying the proclamation described an urn of immortal incense and scrolls of salvific power, offering endless heavenly grace and abolishing guilt and crime for lost souls. It prayed for the nation's lasting peace and blessings.

Greatly pleased, Taizong encouraged the monks to remain steadfast in their devotion and service to Buddha, promising ample rewards for their efforts and merits, assuring them that their hard work would not be in vain.

Here's a paraphrased version of the text that preserves the original meaning:

A grand procession approached the temple, with dignitaries and high-ranking officials clad in finery and adorned with symbols of authority. Banners and flags flapped in the wind, while guardsmen marched in formation, their armor glinting in the sunlight. The emperor, known for his compassion and wisdom, led a delegation of officials as they honored the Buddha with offerings and prayers. The temple itself was a site of breathtaking beauty, with intricate golden sculptures and delicate jade features. The air was thick with the scent of sandalwood incense, and exotic flowers bloomed amidst the elegant furnishings. Priests and monks gathered around, chanting sacred texts and seeking to guide souls from the jaws of suffering.

The ceremony concluded with the priest presenting the emperor with a sacred proclamation, calling for the deliverance of orphaned souls. The text proclaimed the boundless virtue of the Buddha and the universal principle of karma, offering words of hope and solace to those seeking liberation. Emphasizing the importance of compassion and devotion, it encouraged all present to seize this opportunity for spiritual rebirth.

The emperor was visibly moved by the message, and in response, he exhorted the monks to remain steadfast in their duty, vowing to reward their dedication to the Buddha with honor and riches. With sincere conviction, he assured the assembled throng that their efforts would not go unrewarded, and that the blessings they sought would indeed be theirs to enjoy.

Leaning over the balustrade, Nikolai Apollonovich called down in the direction of the entrance-hall, but his call was answered first by silence, then, with excessive distinctness, by an unexpected, reluctant falsetto:

There was no thunder and no lightning after this. This made the Wizard still more anxious, and he burnt more incense and charms and recited more incantations and struck the table as his fourth signal. Instantly the dragons from the four oceans appeared. Sun cried out above their heads, “Aoguang, where do you think you are going?” Aoguang and his companions came forward and bowed. Then Sun told them what he had told the other helpers before, and added, “You helped me before though not successfully. Today I hope you will help me with success.” The Dragon King replied, “I gladly obey your orders.” Sun said, “I beg of you to give me a helping hand. The Wizard’s signal of the fourth performance is now finished. It is now my turn, and I expect you all to help me. I burn no incense and have no signal but my club. When I lift up my spear the first time, I want the wind to blow.” The Mistress of the Wind said, “The wind shall blow.” “When I lift up my spear the second time, I want the clouds to gather.” The Master of the Clouds said, “The clouds shall gather.” “When I lift up my spear the third time, I want thunder and lightning.” The Master of Thunder and Mistress of Lightning said, “You shall have them in abundance.” “When I lift up my spear the fourth time, I want rain.” The Dragon replied, “Your orders shall be obeyed.” “When I lift up my spear the fifth time, I want a clear sky without fail.” Having arranged this, he restored the false Sun and took his place. He shouted out, “Master Wizard, your fourth signal for performance is now over and there has been neither wind, clouds, thunder, lightning, nor rain. Now it is my time to try.” The Wizard came down from his platform altar and went to the gallery by the king, and the Monkey followed him. The king asked the wizard how it was that none of his four attempts were successful. The wizard replied, “The rain king was not at home today.” The Monkey angrily replied, “The dragon gods were all at home, but your Royal Wizard is not sufficiently skilful.” The king then ordered the Buddhist monks to mount the platform. Sun invited the Master to go, but he said he could not command rain.
